questions.scenario: After a cutover, some users still reach the old entry point. The DNS record was changed five minutes ago and the old TTL was one hour. What is the likely explanation?

options.text: Some resolvers still have the old answer cached.
The Node.js backend cannot read its environment variables.
The SQL private endpoint is missing.
The NSG priority order is reversed.

questions.explanation: A previous TTL can keep old answers alive for a while. That is a DNS propagation and caching issue, not an app runtime issue.

questions.scenario: Browsers reach the new entry point, but TLS fails because the certificate only covers `api.devpolaris.com`. What is the narrow fix?

options.text: Bind or issue a certificate that covers `orders.devpolaris.com`.
Change the SQL private DNS zone.
Open all inbound ports on the API subnet.

questions.explanation: The failure names a hostname mismatch. The fix belongs at the certificate and entry-point binding.

questions.scenario: Which checks belong before closing a DNS/TLS incident?

options.text: TLS certificate matches the public hostname.
The entry point routes to a healthy backend.
Only the fact that the DNS record saved successfully.

questions.explanation: Saving a DNS record is not enough. The team needs name resolution, TLS, and backend routing evidence.
